questions.prompt: Why is backtesting on the current S&P 500 misleading?

choices.text: Survivorship bias — today's S&P 500 only contains companies that survived bankruptcy, delisting, and merger to today. Names like Enron, Lehman, WorldCom, GE (kicked out, returned), Kodak — gone or shrunken — aren't in your universe even though they'd have been part of a 2000-2010 backtest. Your strategy sees only the eventual winners and looks great; in real-time you'd have held the failures alongside the winners. Fix: use point-in-time index constituents (Norgate Data, Sharadar, paid vendors) that include delisted names with proper history. Free data (Yahoo, default APIs) almost universally introduces survivorship. Discount any free-data backtest accordingly — your real-life experience will be measurably worse.

questions.explanation: Survivorship — today's index excludes companies that failed. Point-in-time data fixes it.

questions.prompt: Your strategy backtests beautifully at parameters (RSI window=23, threshold=2.7, ATR=14, hold=8). Tested neighboring values: window=22 or 24 collapse. Verdict?

choices.text: Curve-fit — robust strategies have wide parameter plateaus, not knife-edges. The fact that performance collapses with a 1-unit parameter change means you've found a regression-to-the-mean fluke in the historical data, not a stable edge. Live trading will hit conditions different from the precise historical sample your params overfit; the strategy will fail immediately. Fix: re-test with broader parameter sweeps, look for plateaus where multiple nearby values perform well; if no plateau exists, the strategy doesn't have a real edge — only an overfit fit. Round numbers (20, 25, 3) that work across a range are more believable than oddly specific values (23, 2.7) that only work at one spot.

questions.explanation: Curve-fit. Knife-edge param sensitivity = overfitting. Robust strategies have parameter plateaus.
